item.title: Would have probably liked this much more if it wasn’t implied that the SCP caused it
item.content:encoded: It was way to predictable and made the whole thing feel… empty, pointless

item.title: Re: 5000 contest - psul's entry
item.content:encoded:
Click "history" on bottom. scrool to first entry at the bottom and click 'V' to load the page as version 0
As for the 'clues'

+ Show
- Hide

Pataphysics Department confirms Project Chachapoyan complete:

DNA sample viable, and cloning process successful. Subject replacement effected on 14 December.

Analytics investigation concluded as anticipated. Pataphysics Committee has determined not to remove afterword from Agent O'Connor's report – grille cipher pages destroyed, and risk of exposure deemed minimal.

Pataphysics Agent-designate Emma Hastings to commence induction immediately.

item.title: Re: 5000 contest - psul's entry
item.content:encoded: What was the previous ending? I'd like to see if I would've enjoyed the first version more than this one, since I enjoyed this one a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Question.
- (answer?)

It's mentioned that SCP-5002's anomaly only functions on published texts. So how could the grille-cipher function? She only had a manuscript in her cell, not a published work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A friendly reminder that both SCP 5002 and the author have the ability to alter the SCP Universe reality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of my favorite SCPs its simple yet complex! Easy to understand but not to simple to be boring or feel like its talking down to the reader. Take my +1 and have a good day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The perfect thing to read while listening to film noir jazz music. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a sucker for mysteries and SCP's so what's not to love about the two together? Very well written and entertaining read.

+1

item.title: Start off a SCP spin-off series?
item.content:encoded:
I think we should make "The Adventures of Agent Ellen O'Connor" it's own sub-genre of SCPs, written in the same format as this article. I can imagine there being another Agatha Christie-style murder or some-sort of unknown poltergeist SCP terrorizing a Foundation site and O'Connor is called in to investigate.

I really liked O'Connor's character, she's basically a female Hercule Poirot with the fashion sense of David Tennant's Doctor Who (In case anyone is confused by that description, I'm referring to how O'Connor was portrayed in NewScapePro's Minecraft SCP Roleplays). I'll just leave this post here and see what you guys think of the idea.

Here's a theme song that I think will go well with Agent O'Connor

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy shit. Holy shit.

That was a thoroughly unique, thrilling, and beautiful read throughout. The reveal and implications are profound and incredibly well executed.

I'm not gonna continue with in depth reasoning for why I love this scip, but this probably just broke into my top 10 list. You've earned my +1 a thousand times over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a question. If the SRA in 5002's chamber is always functional, then how can her "secondary work" even work?

+1 anyway, really fun to read although the ending baffled me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this. Very unique!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this article but I feel like there's one thing missing. So we found out the officer killed 5002 and was arrested but what happened to the others? One guy joined the serpents hand, another SLEPT with the SCP, and the director PURPOSELY LET A GHOST RAPIST OUT OF CONTAINMENT TO DAMAGE OR POTENTIALLY DESTROY ANOTHER ANOMALOUS OBJECT. At the end of the article the director even seems to be helping O'Connor by telling security to arrest the officer but shouldn't she have also been arrested right there on the spot along with everyone else in the room? In fact I wouldn't say it's too much of a stretch to say that the director would be thankful to the officer for killing the person her whole revenge plot revolved around. I suppose it can be assumed that the others were arrested but it still seems especially strange to me that the director was still so cooperative when she just got exposed for causing multiple containment breaches by purposely turning off SRA's. Or maybe this part of the ending I find strange is a result of 5002's anomaly and 5002 just forgot to write about what happened to the rest of them? If that's the case, guess I typed this whole paragraph for no reason. Well that's all, I honestly did enjoy reading this but I found it strange how the director acted at the end of it.

item.title: Good work!
item.content:encoded: Congrats on 5002. This was a very fun read =)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can I make one suggestion? If you want to ratchet up the authentic Agatha Christie feeling in this story, one small addition could really make that happen: a diagram! Just like in those great old whodunnits, where you'd get a diagram of the train or the mansion or the archeological dig site, with notes about where everyone's bedroom was! I think that would add a nice, classic touch!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this may have launched itself to my #1 favorite 5k entry so far, and I've read over 50 so far. definite Agatha Christie vibes… also I want to watch Clue (even if it's not a horror flick).

what a fun read. the best articles, imo, are ones that are long and so engagingly written that the time flies by, and you're sad when it's over (like taboo, 1730, 4971, 3426, & 3002 off the top of my head, but there are more and some I like even better than those listed). this piece really hit that sweet spot for me.

I read the newer version of it so didn't know about the previous stinger til after reading it and seeking the first comments. they're right — this is way better as it currently stands. :-)

seriously … I need to stop gushing, but thanks for sharing this with us. it's rad.

+ show block
– hide block

And so today I will prove that one of you was her killer!
tickled me. I legitimately laughed out loud. fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: matlock, murder she wrote, and columbo over here! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
godDAMN that went down smooth. one of my favorite SCPs ever.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 with extreme prejudice. THIS is how you do a paranormal murder right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone exposed to too much Matlock and Murder She Wrote as a child, this deeply resonates with me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even though this was an enjoyable read the wording is a bit clunky, like it was inorganic(I don’t know if this was on purpose considering the ending). And it bothers me that you don’t mention how everyone else was punished if at all. Regardless +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great tone and writing throughout, the layered nature of it does it a lot of good. While I don't think this gets at the sense of mystery I want out of 5000, +1 for a wonderful skip in general.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding the appreciation for this line in particular. It's a great one-liner on its own, and a perfect encapsulation of the whole concept of a Foundation-set whodunnit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fuckin' brilliant. This was a thrill ride from start to finish, and while there was of course information hidden from the reader until the final log, I enjoyed being able to piece together small details like who Director May likely was. :D I'm so glad someone wrote something like this.

EDIT: And lest I forget, kudos for creating SCP-blackboxes, one of the scariest monsters on this whole website.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The final twist could be seen coming a mile away, but that's pretty much the only negative thing I have to say about this. It's a tightly-packed, skillfully constructed piece that's executed wonderfully, giving a real Christie feel combined with that tang of lingering implications. And does it really matter that the twist was obvious when it was used in such a sparse, matter-of-fact way to tie a bow of such excellent ambiguity at the end?

Easy +1; this was very enjoyable indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm so pleased you liked that line, Zzuxon. I knew I wanted to include it, and it's a bit shoe-horned in, admittedly. And it took *so* long to work out how to phrase it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The narrative is very reminiscent of the classics of the genre

Thanks! I wanted to go for that feel, and apply it in a Foundation setting, so I'm pleased that people think it works.

I have seen some of the comments on the Chinese translation (where I can't comment, not being a member of the Chinese site) - [*user ahshow] there picks out a bunch of references to Christie novels and characters in this story (eg Hastings, medical blackmailers, hidden lovers). I hate to admit it, but none of those specific references were intended! I hope that doesn't detract from your enjoyment, ahshow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just to note that the clue referred to was references to DNA samples. That has now been removed, as a result of the removal of the related ending.

item.title: Re: 5000 contest - psul's entry
item.content:encoded:
A quick update - I have taken the time to remove the previous ending. Anyone who would like to change their vote, please do so ;)

I have made a couple of consequential changes - removing the clues to the deleted ending, fleshing out the final report slightly, and adding a couple of lines to the last interview. Hopefully I haven't ruined anything that was already there, but very happy to take comments!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could you explain what the clue that you forgot about was? I think I missed it on my read-through and can't find it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everything I loved about this I've seen others discuss, so I'll just say that this:

Officer Lowry: Really. You Sherlock Holmes or something?

Agent O'Connor: Actually, Foundation investigations are rather more difficult. Sherlock Holmes, unlike me, could afford to eliminate the impossible.

is among the coolest damn things I've ever read on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was an interesting one for me, because there was a lot to like while there also being a few very small details that I didn't enjoy. Like Gee said in his review, the line about "everyone has secrets" made me roll my eyes at just how cliché and cheesy it was. In light of the Pataphysics reveal at the end (which I rather enjoyed for the same reasons that Kirby did), I'll concede that it could make some sense that a character would say something this hokey. I'll still argue that the line was the worst offender in the piece, standing out as overdone even in light of your ending.

Overall, this was a lot of fun. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so, so good for 99% of the piece, but I have to join the crowd and say that the final pataphysics collapsible is completely unnecessary. Actually, it nearly wrecked it for me, taking the ending from O’Connor’a haunting, ambiguous note to the SCP equivalent of “it was all a dream.” I’m still going to upvote because this is amazing , but I reeeeally wish the last bit were gone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is by far the best entry i have read thus far, and i really wish it the best of luck in the contest. the only thing i can really say is i wasnt a fan of the pata stuff, but it doesn't take away enough to make me dislike it. upvoted with gusto.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree, the last addendum was avoidable, on the other side, the fact that this is so clearly "fictional" and "standard" is a pro.
+1, had fun

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was enjoyable and had engaging characters. However, I'm no-voting for two reasons. One is the pataphysics addendum a bunch of other people complained about - I think the blurring of the lines between fiction and reality is thematically apparent enough throughout the whole article and the final note without explicitly stating 'yup it's pataphysics'. Second is the murder mystery genre tropes - I found it kind of silly that everyone has some sort of dramatic motive for being the murderer or having some dark secret. I know that this is how these stories work, and it makes sense to write it that way in the article both as an homage to the genre and as part of the broader meta - themes about writing, so I feel bad about listing this as a criticism, but it did stop the article from really clicking with me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was one of the most enjoyable posts on this site that I've ever read! O'Connor was an incredibly believable professional detective who definitely enjoys her job (that she's really good at, but not to the point of incredulity) that, like other commenters have said, recalls Poirot or Benoit Blanc. (We've all got Knives Out on the mind, okay.) Her conclusions were telegraphed just far enough in advance that the extra evidence/deductions she presented naturally progressed from the information that we were given, yet there was still enough suspense to keep me guessing. (of course, i do indeed possess 0 brain cells)

Also, while this doesn't seem to have the ~scope~ of the usual _000 contest entries, I'm personally always a huge fan of humanizing the Foundation and getting a window into their everyday lives and interpersonal relationships. Because of that, I quite like the side of the Foundation you've presented for us.

Bravo! + all the 1's

However - joining the echo chamber in that the stinger seemed pretty unnecessary, although I don't think it detracts from the quality of the article, per se, because the dialogue and the plotting still holds up extremely well despite it. It's already a tight piece of work without the addition.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy moly, this one is good. A tad long, but a bloody good read. You've got my +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a whole lot, and I love that it's one of only a few that I think have gone with a really direct interpretation of the prompt.

I will agree with some previous commentators that I think the rape subplot isn't really relevant to the murder mystery, and also I'd very much like to know what happened to all the other characters afterwards- the whole site is probably facing disciplinary action at this point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty amazing, and among my top three 5000 entries of the contest, but I'm still going to no-vote.

It is written fantastically, and the protagonist actually feels like a gumshoe detective, solving the crime. But like everyone else I feel like the sting at the end undercut the ending prose and any mystery that people might have felt after finishing the article. The unknown endings of the other characters besides the murderer really feels like a lost opportunity to tie up some loose ends, and could be expanded upon more.

_000 entries should be expansive in scope, and delve into hitherto vastly unexplored expanses of the Foundation mythos, like amnestics, history, or end-of-the-world scenarios. This entry, being a tightly-focused-noir-like murder mystery does not fit the "expansive" definition at all. So while I will most definitely upvote after the contest is over, I will save it for now.

But other than that, it is a solid 10/10 article that I love very much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is definitely one of my favourites.
I got hooked as soon as I realised it was going to be a locked room murder, and genuinely couldn't stop reading until the end. It's very well-written, with great characterisation and engaging plot, and the murder itself has just the right amount of clues. The narrative is very reminiscent of the classics of the genre and I really enjoyed it — even more so when I made the connection between the way the story is presented and Emma being a detective writer. I also liked the little depictions of Foudation's personnel's everyday activities, the treatment of humanoid SCPs (Simpson even calls Emma "it" to her face at one point!), test logs and ponderings on the exact workings of the anomaly; all topics I enjoy reading about.

Another thing I want to mention is the SCP's anomaly itself: "writing stuff causes it to come true" isn't a particulary exciting or a novel idea to me, but you developed it (by including the discussion about free will and D-classes reacting to being subjected to anomaly) and turned it into something truly interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Had a feeling where this was going from the beginning, but by golly was it fun. Sometimes you just have to play it straight, and nobody can say it doesn't fit the mystery theme.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a masterpiece. I appreciate the divisiveness of the ending and discourage you to go against your gut feeling. If this gets in the 5000 slot, the site will have taken a giant leap forward.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
Thanks very much for the feedback, Gee. I've updated those consistency points (I can't believe I missed a "Ms." after working so hard against my usual preference for all those "Dr."s).

Agent O'Connor: This is the Foundation, doctor. Everyone has secrets.

I take your point about this line, and I wasn't absolutely confident about it. But I wanted to mix between straight segues (like "do you know why he ended up as a D-Class?") and what I'd call "ad-break" lines to end the interviews, and this felt like the latter. If I think of something stronger, I'll use it, but I do think something a bit "on the nose" is needed to ensure readers open the next collapsible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for your comments, planet Jane, and I'm pleased that you liked it overall. I'll appreciate the detailed points you make, and I've responded below.

+ show block
– hide block

I really do not think the rape subplot … adds anything at all … I don't think simple shock value (which it strikes me as here) justifies its inclusion. I really think you can write that out and make the whole thing a lot more digestible.

You make the fair point that this may be a failure of imagination on my part. I wanted the SCP to be a threat (because it justifies the Site Director's use as a tool of revenge, and also because the SCP doesn't get much screentime, so needs to be an obvious threat), and I wanted the nature of that threat to be something other than murder (because I wanted variety among my characters, and the D-Class murderer was already there). Choosing sexual assault was probably a cheap decision in that respect, and it felt like it fit the "walking through walls" anomaly in terms of invasion of space.

I'm open to discuss another way of providing the SCP with the same level of threat, and I'll think about it further myself. I don't know that I can remove them entirely without making major changes to the story, as the Site Director and doctor's plots depend indirectly on this SCP's role in proceedings. But let me know any thoughts.

Going farther and involving the Pataphysics Dept. just strikes me as serious overkill, it's kind of the same fundamental problem as ending a story with someone waking up from sleeping.

As noted to Taffeta above, I agree with this, and I may well decide to nix this ending. But I do have some affection for it, in part for the very reason you identify - this is a huge anticlimax. I like that fact, as a playful subversion of expectations. My first two endings are a typical murder mystery ending, followed by a typical Foundation story ending. The Pataphysics ending is exactly the thing that SCP-5000 herself warned against - it's effectively "the aliens committing the crime".

The other reason I like this ending is that I think it adds to the contest theme, and adds some fridge logic to the piece. While "Pataphysics did it" resolves one mystery, it begs more questions (as SCP-5000 also alluded to) - exactly how and when did they substitute the clone, and how could the events as we've seen them play out with a clone in the containment chamber?

I may yet change the ending (and perhaps beef up O'Connor's note in compensation), but I wanted to highlight why I put it there in the first place, just for discussion purposes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is excellently written and the ending really does leave me thinking. Having just seen Knives Out, I was really in the mood for this kind of story. Well done!

Just one error that I can see, in the first test with the D-class, the first mention of D-2825 in the transcript is mistakenly put as D-4986. Under Investigation Materials - Volume 3.
-Corrected. Again, well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a wonderfully-executed whodunnit, which perfectly captures the mystery theme. I'm genuinely impressed at how many characters are involved — and how neatly everyone's story/testimony comes together to build towards the reveal. No stone is unturned, and not a word is wasted — it's quite economic despite it's length.

I'll jump on the bandwagon in voting against the hidden ending — it does feel like it takes the piss out of O'Connor's statement to a degree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved this article. The clues, the plot and the overall mystery are very well-structured, and the piece was certainly engaging. Even though it's pretty long, the final reveal is easily worth it.

However, I'll have to support other's statements regarding the last, hidden collapsible: I can't help but feel that it devalues the presented story a bit and adds unnecessary complexity. The agent's final report that comes before it serves as a great hook and leaves a lot of questions that fit the theme perfectly, it welcomes the reader to draw their own conclusions from the piece. But then the Pataphysics thing comes up and… Just states what the hidden storyline is. Honestly, I feel like the article would read much, much better if the final collapsible was just fully removed. But it didn't ruin the piece for me, and I find the amount put into it very admirable. Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
uuuuugggggh idkidkidk.

I've written 2 reviews and deleted them. I really like this entry, but I don't think it has the feel of something that would be in the 5000 spot. I may come back and +1 after I read the rest of the entries, but I can say that I will for-sure at least +1 after the contest is over. A lovely read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed the piece overall - it's a murder mystery done very, very well - but I dooo have some thoughts about that last collapsible.

Namely, while I think it might slightly weaken the mindfuckery of the bits before it, I do really like that simultaneously pays off the earlier DNA clue, pays homage to a classic "the victim isn't dead" twist, AND hints at a greater storytelling opportunity, with future works involving Emma.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you, Taffeta - I'm pleased that you enjoyed it, and that you felt it was sufficiently representative of what I was trying to do. Without underselling the amount of work that went into this, it was enjoyable to write in a way that writing isn't always - anyone who likes setting up dominoes, then watching them fall, will get what I mean.

I don't really care to have an in-universe explanation that feels somewhat like an attempt to ward off joyless nitpickers moreso than a coherent twist.

As a joyless nitpicker myself, I object to that sentiment! :)

Or perhaps I can put it this way: "I'm a joyless nitpicker, and I vote."

That said, I think you've absolutely skewered me on part of why I added that ending in. And seeing my motives exposed like that does incline me somewhat to removing it. That said, I would be interested in hearing from anyone with the contrary view - do you prefer the piece ending with the final addendum? Would it change your vote if it was removed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great stuff! To be entirely honest, I was really apprehensive with this entry once I got past the containment details and realized the nature of the skip. Figured things were going to get very meta, very fast. But as I kept on, the fantastic construction of the entry, paired with serious professional-grade writing swayed my opinion.

Spoilers
Collapse me

Just to put my finger on the scale re: the SCP who stares at goats and the pataphysics blurb. While I can't say I particularly enjoyed reading its dialogue, the interview log was a great red herring that made the final explanation an even better reveal. When I got to the pataphysics section, I'll agree with some of the other comments - I feel like it detracted from the grand mystery that you presented. Was the entire event real, or a theatrical suicide directed by a reality-manipulating mystery author? Does it matter when the whole thing isn't real anyway?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is probably the first entry where I came away thinking "fuck me this is good". The construction of the murder mystery is superb and… well I'll put the rest in a spoiler collapsible for good measure:

spoiers
– hide block

The bit with the guy who walks through walls was, at first hilarious, and then incredibly creepy.

And like, normally I'd be against something like the pataphysics reveal because we already have so much metafiction, but the thing is that, given the nature of the anomaly the pataphysics question lingered in my head and was never touched, so it didn't feel like an article about pataphysics, which is very good. But then on top of that… THE FUCKING DNA CLUE THAT I HAD TOTALLY FORGOTTEN ABOUT UNTIL THE END HOLY SHIT.

Like, I had noticed the DNA bit when it came up both times but there was so much time between those mentions and the reveal that I forgot. And then remembering that clue just felt so goddamn satisfying oh my god.

psul, you're going to make me look bad, damn it. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+Opinions (spoilers)
– hide block

I'll admit, this article was a bit slow to start, but once all the pieces were put into place and it was time for the grand unveiling, the amount of times I whispered "holy fuck" and "no way" may just be unmatched by any other article in recent memory. A masterfully executed exhibition of the contest theme with strong, engaging characters all throughout — even with the (in my opinion) somewhat questionable Pataphysics stinger, such a well-written mystery deserves an easy upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
O'Connor's voice as the protagonist of a mystery novel is really strong here, and the mystery is satisfying and fun.

Unfortunately, the sting kind of undercuts it! It takes away from O'Connor's conclusions as the finale, and makes her "my will is not my own" comments less important since pataphysics is going "of course not lol" and it still just kind of feels out of place. I think this is a stronger piece without it. If you think something needs to go there, I'd suggest a denouement on what happened to all the rest of the characters: what happened with Simpson being outed as a Serpent's Hand operative? What happened to doctor Yau after it was revealed she was sleeping with an anomaly? What happened after it was revealed that May and Grossenbacher were fucking with security?

I'm upvoting, and I like this one a lot - it does justice to the genre, but I can't help but feel that it shoots itself in the foot at the end.

item.title: Interesting take on murder mystery
item.content:encoded: This scp has a mixture of a somewhat overdone trope of the murder mystery seen a thousand times before and a bit of alan wake mixed in as both were capable of manipulating reality through writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great submission. Loved the murder mystery format of the post, will you ever make a follow up?

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
[+ Opinion (Spoilers Probably)]
– hide block

God, most of this is absolutely amazing and I was hooked all the way through. Yes, it's a murder mystery played fairly straight, but is that an issue? Not for me. As far as the writing goes, it was generally really good. I have a few extremely minor nitpicks/opinions, though they're by no means things that affected my opinion any noticeable amount.

Today, with one exception, this room contains all of the people who were present in Wing G on the night that Ms Hastings was killed.

Though (and I've checked this) opinions on whether to add a period after Ms do very, due in part to the fact that it's not actually an abbreviation of anything, the majority of sources I checked (and my personal preference) seem to favour adding one. It seems to help with consistency, especially when it's so close to the mentions of "Dr." I definitely wouldn't hold it against you if you were to keep it without one though.

SCP-5000 was killed between 1am and 3:00am last night.

This is the most apparent offender of the minor issues with consistency in how you've written time in this. I have no real preference for how it's written, though maybe look through a little to check it's consistent.

Agent O'Connor: This is the Foundation, doctor. Everyone has secrets.

This is the one line of dialogue that left a slightly sour taste in my mouth. It's both extremely cliché and overly foreshadowing the end sequence of this; honestly I feel it only detracts from the piece.

I admire your character development, no two characters felt overly similar and none were especially shallow. While I've seen contrasting opinions on the rape part, I feel that the interviewer's emotional reaction to it added a whole layer of depth to the character. I think it maybe went on about one interview too long, though that's not especially helpful as I can't think of one that could really be cut.

As for the controversial pataphysical section. I'll admit that I somehow managed to miss it on my first runthrough, and didn't feel the piece was missing anything at the time. Therefore, I agree with some of the other commenters that it came across as unnecessary and may have dulled the ending for me a little. Not enough to change my vote, but enough that I noticed.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to gee0765 or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm no-voting for now, despite liking this overall, because I have two big complaints. I offer this comment because I'm hoping to see what is in my opinion a good skip become a great one.

I'll start with the positive: the scope and length here are more than enough to make this a good SCP-5000, in my opinion. I like that it's an obvious interpretation of the theme but one that goes whole hog. It's essentially a short detective novella set within the SCP Foundation. You genuinely deserve a lot of credit for pulling that off and making it compelling.

My main points of complaint are this:

-I really do not think the rape subplot with our unnumbered SCP who can walk through walls adds anything at all. Rape as a narrative device is something to be used with caution and I don't think simple shock value (which it strikes me as here) justifies its inclusion. I really think you can write that out and make the whole thing a lot more digestible.

-I want to back up Taffeta here:

In re: the ending, I don't particularly care for the Pataphysics reveal because you know what, not every piece of genre work on the site needs a cheeky 'eh? eh? eh?' to justify its existence. I think I can suspend my disbelief and accept that this is a little ''''''unrealistic'''''' when it's this well-written already. I don't really care to have an in-universe explanation that feels somewhat like an attempt to ward off joyless nitpickers moreso than a coherent twist.

But also escalate it. Emma's ability will already leave readers in doubt as to whether Lowry *really* acted of his own volition. Pointing that out at the end once is fine—and very much in-character for the Foundation. Going farther and involving the Pataphysics Dept. just strikes me as serious overkill, it's kind of the same fundamental problem as ending a story with someone waking up from sleeping. This is just a more involved iteration of that. You need to trust your fiction to stand on its own two legs, and if "joyless nitpickers" as Taffeta colorfully calls them want to complain about it, that is their business.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Murder on the Site-06 Express
